perturbation.original_text: For the purposes hereof, Proprietary Information shall be any documents, materials or information that (i) is in tangible form and clearly marked as being proprietary, confidential or sensitive and (ii) is received by one party to this Agreement ("Recipient") from the other party to this Agreement ("Transmitter").
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes hereof, Proprietary Information shall be any documents, materials or information that (i) is in tangible form, regardless of marking, and (ii) is received by one party to this Agreement ("Recipient") from the other party to this Agreement ("Transmitter").
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'clearly marked' requirement broadens the definition of Proprietary Information beyond what is typically protected, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that often require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, including marking.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets as information subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Removing the 'clearly marked' requirement removes the need for reasonable marking efforts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'clearly marked' requirement for proprietary information, which the Law Explanation states equates to removing the need for reasonable marking efforts to maintain secrecy as part of trade secret protection under UTSA. The Perturbation Explanation similarly states that removing the marking requirement can conflict with trade secret laws that require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, including marking. While the provided snippets are not helpful, the removal of the 'clearly marked' requirement, when coupled with the explanations, suggests a weakening of the effort to maintain secrecy, potentially contradicting established trade secret law requirements. This represents a deviation from legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: SELLER shall only tender for acceptance those items that conform to the requirements of this Agreement. Unisys reserves the right to inspect or test any supplies or services that have been tendered for acceptance. Unisys may require repair or replacement of nonconforming supplies or reperformance of nonperforming services at no additional cost. Unisys agrees to exercise its post acceptance rights (1) within a reasonable time after the defect was discovered or should have been discovered; and (2) before any substantial change occurs in the condition of the item, unless the change is due to the defect in the item.
perturbation.changed_text: SELLER shall only tender for acceptance those items that conform to the requirements of this Agreement. Unisys reserves the right to inspect or test any supplies or services that have been tendered for acceptance. Unisys may require repair or replacement of nonconforming supplies or reperformance of nonperforming services at no additional cost. Unisys agrees to exercise its post acceptance rights only (1) after 180 days after the defect was discovered or should have been discovered; and (2) after a substantial change occurs in the condition of the item, even if the change is not due to the defect in the item.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the time frame from 'reasonable time' to a fixed 180 days and requiring 'substantial change' before exercising rights makes the acceptance terms unreasonably restrictive, potentially violating implied warranties and the UCC.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-607 requires the buyer to notify the seller of breach within a reasonable time after discovery, or be barred from any remedy. Delaying the exercise of post-acceptance rights for 180 days is almost certainly not a reasonable time.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-607. Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach After Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-607. Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach After Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over. (1) The buyer must pay at the contract rate for any goods accepted. (2) Acceptance of goods by the buyer precludes rejection of the goods accepted and if made with knowledge of a non-conformity cannot be revoked because of it unless the acceptance was on the reasonable assumption that the non-conformity would be seasonably cured but acceptance does not of itself impair any other remedy provided by this Article for non-conformity. (3)Where a tender has been accepted (a) the buyer must within a reasonable time after he discovers or should have discovered any breach notify the seller of breach or be barred from any remedy; and (b) if the claim is one for infringement or the like (subsection (3) of Section 2-312 ) and the buyer is sued as a result of such a breach he must so notify the seller within a reasonable time after he receives notice of the litigation or be barred from any remedy over for liability established by the litigation. (4) The burden is on the buyer to establish any breach with respect to the goods accepted. (5) Where the buyer is sued for breach of a warranty or other obligation for which his seller is answerable over (a) he may give his seller written notice of the litigation. If the notice states that the seller may come in and defend and that if the seller does not do so he will be bound in any action against him by his buyer by any determination of fact common to the two litigations, then unless the seller after seasonable receipt of the notice does come in and defend he is so bound. (b) if the claim is one for infringement or the like
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts UCC 2-607, as explained in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text requires Unisys to exercise its post-acceptance rights within a 'reasonable time,' aligning with the UCC's 'reasonable time' requirement for notifying the seller of a breach. The changed text extends this period to a fixed 180 days and introduces a requirement for 'substantial change' before exercising these rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section (3)(a), states that the buyer must notify the seller of a breach 'within a reasonable time after he discovers or should have discovered any breach or be barred from any remedy.' Setting a fixed 180-day period, and requiring a 'substantial change' undermines this 'reasonable time' requirement, potentially barring Unisys from remedies even if a breach is identified much earlier. This creates a legal contradiction by deviating from the UCC's mandate for timely notification.

perturbation.original_text: SELLER agrees to comply with all applicable Federal, State and local laws, executive orders, rules and regulations applicable to its performance under this Agreement. SELLER agrees to comply with the following Federal Acquisition Regulation clauses, which shall be deemed incorporated by reference:
perturbation.changed_text: SELLER agrees to comply with all applicable Federal laws, executive orders, rules and regulations applicable to its performance under this Agreement. SELLER agrees to comply with the following Federal Acquisition Regulation clauses, which shall be deemed incorporated by reference:
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the requirement to comply with 'State and local laws,' the clause creates a contradiction. For example, if a state requires a specific business license, the seller would not contractually be required to obtain it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Supremacy Clause of the US Constitution, Article VI
perturbation.law_explanation: Even if a contract does not explicitly state compliance with state and local laws, businesses are still obligated to follow them. By omitting these laws, the contract creates a false impression that they can be ignored, which is untrue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the requirement for the SELLER to comply with 'State and local laws,' which creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that even if a contract does not explicitly state compliance with state and local laws, businesses are still obligated to follow them. By omitting these laws, the contract creates a false impression that they can be ignored, which is untrue. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states "This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding." This reinforces the idea that state laws are still binding.

perturbation.original_text: All correspondence or notifications required under this Agreement shall be addressed as follows: UNISYS Corporation
perturbation.changed_text: All correspondence or notifications required under this Agreement shall be addressed as follows: To be determined by the BUYER at a later date.
perturbation.explanation: Removing a defined point of contact for legal notifications is problematic. Notice is a vital component of contract law. Leaving the point of contact open implies any notification is valid.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding notice
perturbation.law_explanation: A fundamental aspect of contract law dictates proper notice between parties involved in an agreement. Proper notice includes communicating to a specific contact. Removing the designated contact for UNISYS invalidates a key condition.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts fundamental contract law principles regarding proper notice. While the provided law snippets are not directly helpful, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both emphasize that designating a specific contact is a vital component of providing legally sound notice. Removing the designated contact, as the modified text does, creates a legal contradiction by potentially invalidating future attempts to provide notice. This misalignment of terminology deviates from legally recognized practices, making the contract potentially unenforceable.
